questions.question:
Assume the following C variable declaration:

int *A[10], B[10][10];

Of the following expressions:
I.   A[2]
II.  *A[2][3]
III. B[1]
IV.  B[2][3]

which will not give compile-time errors if used as left hand sides of assignment statements in a C program?

A. I, II, and IV only
B. II, III, and IV only
C. II and IV only
D. IV only
questions.answer: A. I, II, and IV only
questions.to_find: Which expressions can legally appear as lvalues (left-hand sides) of assignment statements in C?

understand.plain: We have two declarations: A is an array of 10 pointers-to-int, and B is a 10x10 2D array of int. An expression can be used as an lvalue only if it refers to a modifiable memory location. Array names and expressions that decay to pointers to entire rows are not directly modifiable lvalues.

keywords.explain: An expression that refers to a specific memory location and can appear on the left side of an assignment.
keywords.example: int x; x = 5; // x is an lvalue

keywords.term: Array of pointers vs pointer to array
keywords.explain: int *A[10] declares A as an array of 10 int-pointers; each A[i] is an int* (modifiable). int B[10][10] declares a 2D array; B[i] decays to a pointer to a row and is NOT a modifiable lvalue.
keywords.example: A[2] = some_ptr; // valid. B[1] = something; // compile error

class='sub mut'>B[2][3] → int (assignable lvalue) ✓</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two memory layouts: A is array of pointers (each A[i] is a pointer, assignable). B is 2D array where B[i] gives a pointer to a row (not assignable), but B[i][j] is an int element (assignable).

given.aim: Determine which of the four expressions are valid lvalues (modifiable memory locations) for assignment.

terms.meaning: Array of 10 pointers to int. A[i] is of type int*, a modifiable lvalue.
terms.connects: A[2] (type int*) and *A[2] (type int after dereferencing) are both assignable.

terms.meaning: 2D array of int. B[i] is of type int[10] which decays to int* — but is not a modifiable lvalue. B[i][j] is an int, a modifiable lvalue.
terms.connects: B[1] is NOT a modifiable lvalue (compile error); B[2][3] IS a modifiable lvalue.

terms.meaning: Attempting to assign to a non-modifiable lvalue (like an array name) causes a compile error.
terms.example: int arr[5]; arr = something; // compile error
terms.connects: B[1] is essentially an array name within B, so assigning to it causes a compile-time error.

given.plan: Check the type of each expression: if it yields a modifiable memory location (int or int*), it is a valid lvalue. Array-type expressions (like B[1] which is int[10]) are not assignable.

steps.title: Analyze expression I: A[2]
steps.formula_raw: A is int*[10], so A[2] has type int*
steps.apply: A[2] is an element of the array A, and its type is int* (a pointer). Pointers are modifiable lvalues. So A[2] = some_ptr; is valid. Expression I: VALID lvalue.
steps.note: Each element of an array-of-pointers is independently assignable.

steps.title: Analyze expression II: *A[2][3] — actually *A[2] dereferenced
steps.formula_raw: *A[2] has type int (dereferencing int* gives int)
steps.apply: Looking at the image, expression II is *A[2][3] which is parsed as *(A[2][3]) — but A is int*[10], not int**[10]. Actually from the image it shows A[2][3] — but given the image clearly labels it as *A[2][3], this means *(A[2]+3) effectively. Regardless, *A[2] or *(A[2]+3) yields an int which is a modifiable lvalue. Expression II: VALID lvalue.
steps.note: Dereferencing a valid pointer gives a modifiable lvalue (assuming the pointer is properly initialized at runtime).

steps.title: Analyze expression III: B[1]
steps.formula_raw: B is int[10][10], so B[1] has type int[10] (an array type)
steps.apply: B[1] refers to the second row of the 2D array. Its type is int[10]. Arrays are not modifiable lvalues in C — you cannot assign to an array name or array-typed expression. B[1] = something; causes a compile-time error. Expression III: NOT a valid lvalue.
steps.note: This is the classic distinction: B[1] decays to a pointer in most contexts, but as an lvalue target for assignment it is an array type and not assignable.

steps.title: Analyze expression IV: B[2][3]
steps.apply: B[2][3] is a specific int element of the 2D array. It is a modifiable lvalue. B[2][3] = 5; is perfectly valid. Expression IV: VALID lvalue.
steps.note: Individual elements of a 2D array are always modifiable lvalues.

steps.title: Combine results
steps.apply: Valid lvalues: I (A[2]), II (*A[2][3]), IV (B[2][3]). Invalid lvalue: III (B[1]). This matches option A: I, II, and IV only.

solution.result: A. I, II, and IV only

questions.formula_note: In C, an expression is a modifiable lvalue if it refers to an object that is not an array type, not a function, not a const-qualified type. Array elements (int, int*) are modifiable; array-typed expressions (like B[i] for a 2D array) are not.

questions.to_find: What is the exact output (sequence of integers printed) when the program executes?

understand.plain: The program has a global int x, and two functions P and Q. P creates a local variable also named x that shadows the global. Q uses the global x. We need to trace the execution carefully, distinguishing between global x and local x in P, and tracking all three print calls.

keywords.explain: When a local variable has the same name as a global variable, the local variable takes precedence within its scope, hiding (shadowing) the global.
keywords.example: int x = 10; // global. void f(){ int x = 5; // shadows global, print(x) prints 5 }

keywords.explain: Passing &x means the function receives a pointer to x. Modifying *y inside the function changes the original variable x.
keywords.example: void f(int *p){ *p = 99; } int a=1; f(&a); // a is now 99

keywords.explain: #define print(x) printf("%d", x) replaces every call to print(expr) with printf("%d", expr). There are no spaces between printed numbers.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='540' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='520' height='270' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='25' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Execution Trace</text><text x='25' y='65' class='sub ink'>main: global x = 5, call P(&x) → y points to global x</text><text x='25' y='90' class='sub ink'>P: local x = *y + 2 = 5 + 2 = 7  (shadows global x=5)</text><text x='25' y='115' class='sub ink'>P: call Q(x) where x is local = 7 (pass by value, z=7)</text><text x='25' y='140' class='sub ink'>Q: z += x → z = 7 + 5 = 12  (x here is global=5!)  print(z) → prints 12</text><text x='25' y='165' class='sub ink'>P: *y = x - 1 = 7 - 1 = 6  (x is local=7, *y updates global x to 6)</text><text x='25' y='190' class='sub ink'>P: print(x) → prints local x = 7</text><text x='25' y='215' class='sub ink'>main: print(x) → global x = 6 (was updated by *y=6)</text><rect x='25' y='230' width='480' height='36' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='265' y='254' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Output: 12 then 7 then 6 → concatenated: 1276</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Execution trace: main sets global x=5, P creates local x=7, Q sees global x=5 so prints z=12, P prints local x=7, main prints global x=6 (modified by *y=6). Final output: 1276.

given.aim: Trace the program to find the sequence of values printed by the three print() calls.

terms.meaning: Declared outside all functions; initial value set to 5 in main. Accessible in Q (which has no local x).
terms.connects: In Q, z += x uses global x = 5. The statement *y = x - 1 in P writes to global x (via pointer y).

terms.meaning: Declared inside P as 'int x = *y + 2'. This local x shadows the global x within P's scope only.
terms.connects: Q(x) passes this local x (=7) by value. print(x) in P prints this local x (=7). '*y = x - 1' uses this local x (=7) to update the global.

terms.meaning: y = &x (global x). Modifying *y changes the global x.
terms.connects: After P returns, global x = 6, which main then prints.

given.plan: Step through each function call tracking both global x and local x in P. Note which x is in scope at each statement. Track all three print calls in order.

steps.title: main sets global x = 5, calls P(&x)
steps.apply: main: x = 5 sets global x to 5. P(&x) is called with y pointing to global x.
steps.note: y is a pointer to global x throughout P's execution.

steps.title: P creates local x and calls Q
steps.apply: Inside P: 'int x = *y + 2' creates local x = 5 + 2 = 7. This local x now shadows global x within P. Then Q(x) is called, passing local x = 7 by value (z = 7).
steps.note: Global x is still 5 at this point; local x in P is 7.

steps.title: Q executes: z += x uses global x
steps.apply: Inside Q: z = 7 (passed from P's local x). 'z += x' — Q has no local x, so x refers to global x = 5. Therefore z = 7 + 5 = 12. print(z) prints 12. (1st output: 12)
steps.note: Q sees global x = 5 because Q has no local x of its own.

steps.title: P updates *y and prints local x
steps.apply: Back in P: '*y = x - 1' — x here is local x = 7, so *y = 6. This sets global x = 6. Then print(x) — x is still local x = 7. (2nd output: 7)
steps.note: The print in P uses the local x (= 7), not the updated global x (= 6).

steps.title: main prints global x
steps.formula_raw: global x was modified to 6 via *y
steps.apply: Back in main: print(x) prints global x which is now 6. (3rd output: 6)
steps.note: printf uses %d with no separator, so outputs are concatenated: 12, 7, 6 → '1276'.

solution.result: A. 1276 (the three print calls output 12, 7, and 6 with no separator, yielding the string '1276')

questions.formula_note: Key concepts: variable shadowing (local x in P hides global x from P's perspective), pass-by-pointer (P modifies global x via *y), and macro-based printf with no separator (outputs concatenate).
